quote: watching King Charles II die at Whitehall Palace
subject: charles-ii
note: The death of Charles II in February 1685 marked a major turning point in English history, leading to the succession of his Catholic brother, James II.

quote: pursuing his quest for the Philosophic Mercury
subject: quicksilver-mercury
note: In alchemy, the Philosophic Mercury was a legendary substance required to create the Philosopher's Stone and transmute base metals into gold.

quote: occult studies of the Book of Revelation and the Temple of Solomon
subject: apocalypticism
note: Isaac Newton spent decades studying biblical prophecy and the dimensions of Solomon's Temple, believing they contained encoded mathematical secrets about the universe.

quote: attempted to indict Nell Gwyn as a prostitute
subject: Nell Gwyn
note: A famous actress and the most popular mistress of Charles II; she was a Protestant folk hero often contrasted with the King's Catholic mistresses.

quote: commenced hanging Catholics for being part of a supposed Popish Plot
subject: The Popish Plot
note: A fictitious conspiracy concocted by Titus Oates in 1678, alleging a Jesuit plan to assassinate Charles II, which led to a wave of anti-Catholic hysteria and executions.

quote: William of Orange
subject: William III of England
note: The Dutch Stadtholder and Protestant champion who would eventually take the English throne during the Glorious Revolution.

quote: the Duke of Monmouth (who was Protestant) had been encouraged to parade around
subject: monmouth
note: The eldest illegitimate son of Charles II; as a Protestant, he was seen by many as a preferable alternative to the Catholic James II.

quote: exclude James (and every other Catholic) from the throne
subject: The Exclusion Crisis
note: A political struggle from 1679–1681 where Parliament sought to bar the King's brother James from succession due to his Catholicism.

quote: whipped up by Sir Roger L’Estrange
subject: Roger L'Estrange
note: A prominent Tory press censor and pamphleteer who fiercely attacked the Whigs and defenders of the Popish Plot.

quote: these Whigs (as L’Estrange libelled them) had voted for Exclusion
subject: Whigs and Tories
note: The two emerging political factions: Whigs supported Exclusion and parliamentary supremacy, while Tories supported the divine right of kings and the legitimate succession.

quote: embed a few square blocks of Cartesian rationality
subject: René Descartes
note: Refers to the philosophy of René Descartes, emphasizing logical, grid-like order and mathematical deduction, here applied to urban planning.

quote: involved in a Rye House Plot to put Monmouth on the throne
subject: The Rye House Plot
note: A 1683 Whig conspiracy to assassinate Charles II and James; its discovery led to the execution or exile of many leading opposition figures.

quote: Thirteen hundred Quakers, Barkers, and other Dissenters
subject: Dissenters
note: Protestants who refused to conform to the Church of England; they faced significant legal persecution during the Restoration.

quote: Charles II’s reign. He was the King, he loved France and hated Puritans
subject: Charles II
note: The 'Merry Monarch' whose restoration to the throne in 1660 ended the Interregnum. His pro-French sympathies and religious tensions are central to the political backdrop of the novel.

quote: the boat that carried the chirurgeons
subject: Chirurgeons
note: An archaic term for surgeons. At the time, surgery was a manual craft distinct from the academic profession of physicians, often associated with barbers.

quote: towards London Bridge... penetrating its turbulent arches
subject: Old London Bridge
note: The bridge acted as a partial dam; the narrow arches caused the Thames to 'shoot' through with dangerous force, a phenomenon known as 'shooting the bridge.'

quote: the night in 1670 when he’d come to Whitehall in Pepys’s carriage
subject: Samuel Pepys
note: A naval administrator and famous diarist whose writings provide the most detailed account of life in Restoration London.

quote: the only witness had been Cromwell’s severed head
subject: Oliver Cromwell
note: After the Restoration, Cromwell's body was exhumed and posthumously executed; his head was displayed on a spike at Westminster Hall for decades.

quote: letting a few Barkers and Quakers out of prison
subject: Quakers
note: The Religious Society of Friends; a dissenting group that faced heavy persecution and imprisonment under the Clarendon Code for refusing to swear oaths or attend the Church of England.

quote: Whitehall Palace was, in the end, a House: the house of a Family
subject: Whitehall Palace
note: The main residence of English monarchs in London from 1530 until 1698; it was a sprawling, disorganized complex of over 1,500 rooms.

quote: the subject of Humours. It all began with an apoplectical fit
subject: Humorism
note: The ancient medical system (Galenism) still prevalent in the 17th century, which held that health was governed by the balance of four fluids: blood, phlegm, yellow bile, and black bile.

quote: the Doctor found a vein—the King bade farewell to a pint or two
subject: Bloodletting
note: A standard medical practice used to 'balance' the humors by removing blood, often via lancets or leeches; it frequently weakened patients further.

quote: cupping him all over to draw out other poisons through the skin
subject: Cupping
note: A treatment where heated glass cups were applied to the skin to create a vacuum, believed to draw out 'bad' humors or stimulate blood flow.

quote: my full authority as Secretary of the Royal Society
subject: The Royal Society
note: The premier scientific institution of the era, founded in 1660 to promote 'experimental philosophy.'

quote: the Sons of Asclepius were finished with him
subject: Asclepius
note: The Greek god of medicine and healing; his name is used here as a literary reference to the medical profession.

quote: the Elixir Proprietalis LeFebure
subject: Elixir Proprietatis
note: A famous alchemical medicine originally attributed to Paracelsus, consisting of aloes, myrrh, and saffron; Nicaise Le Febure was a noted chemist to Charles II.

quote: diagnosed a fever. They gave him a royal dose of the Elixir Proprietalis LeFebure.
subject: Elixir Proprietalis
note: A common 17th-century medicine consisting of aloes, myrrh, and saffron dissolved in alcohol; the 'LeFebure' version refers to Nicaise Le Febure, a famous royal chemist.

quote: the bleeders and purgers are upon us!
subject: Humoral Medicine
note: The traditional medical theory that health was governed by four 'humours' (blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile); treatment often involved bloodletting (bleeding) or emetics (purging) to restore balance.

quote: President, to yours as Secretary, of the Royal Society
subject: The Royal Society
note: The preeminent scientific institution of the era, where many of the novel's characters interact.

quote: watermen on yonder dock had all turned their backs on the Privy Stairs
subject: Privy Stairs
note: The private entrance from the River Thames into Whitehall Palace, used by the King and high-ranking officials.

quote: carrying those chirurgeons
subject: Chirurgeons
note: An archaic term for surgeons; at the time, they were often considered lower in status than physicians because they performed manual labor.

quote: Enoch Root made phosphorus from horse urine?
subject: Discovery of Phosphorus
note: Phosphorus was first isolated in 1669 by Hennig Brand by boiling down large quantities of urine; it was a sensation in the scientific community due to its glow.

quote: Republicanism makes perfect sense, seems to work well in Holland
subject: Dutch Republic
note: The United Provinces of the Netherlands, which operated as a republic during this era, providing a political alternative to the absolute monarchies of England and France.

quote: Charles is like Comstock and James is like Anglesey
subject: James II
note: The brother of Charles II and heir to the throne, whose Catholicism and perceived absolutism were highly controversial.

quote: Suppose that, God forbid, Newton died. Who would replace him?
subject: Isaac Newton
note: The central scientific figure of the age, known for his work on gravity, calculus, and optics.

quote: Hooke, or maybe Leibniz.
subject: Robert Hooke
note: A brilliant polymath and curator of experiments for the Royal Society, often a rival to Newton.

quote: Hooke, or maybe Leibniz.
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: A German philosopher and mathematician who independently co-invented calculus.

quote: Recently Leibniz has been refining his system of metaphysics
subject: Monadology
note: Leibniz's metaphysical theory that the universe is made of 'monads'—simple substances that perceive the universe from their own unique perspective.

quote: Hooke peering through his Microscope or Newton through his Reflecting Telescope
subject: Scientific Instruments
note: Hooke's 'Micrographia' revolutionized biology, while Newton's reflecting telescope solved issues of chromatic aberration found in earlier lens-based telescopes.

quote: Dr. Leibniz has been saying some very rude things about Descartes lately
subject: René Descartes
note: The French philosopher whose mechanical view of the universe Leibniz and others were beginning to challenge.

quote: Brevis Demonstratio Erroris Memorabilis Cartesii et Alio-rum Circa Legem Naturalem
subject: Brevis Demonstratio
note: A 1686 paper by Leibniz where he argued that Descartes' laws of motion were incorrect regarding the conservation of 'force' (kinetic energy).

quote: between Jesuits on the one hand, and Puritans on t’other
subject: Religious Conflict in England
note: The political tension between the Catholic influence (Jesuits) favored by James II and the radical Protestantism (Puritans) that had fueled the English Civil War.

quote: clownish sizar at Trinity
subject: Sizar
note: A student at Cambridge (like Trinity College) who received an allowance or free tuition in exchange for performing menial tasks.

quote: the King’s Apartments... King’s Bedchamber... Charles II
subject: Charles II
note: The King of England, Scotland, and Ireland from 1660 to 1685. The passage depicts his final illness in February 1685.

quote: that mad Papist the Duke of York
subject: James II
note: The younger brother of Charles II and heir to the throne. His conversion to Catholicism was a major source of political and religious tension in England.

quote: likening one to Asclepius and the other to Hippocrates
subject: Ancient Greek Medicine
note: Asclepius was the Greek god of medicine, and Hippocrates is considered the father of Western medicine; these are high classical compliments for the attending physicians.

quote: Whitehall... Privy Stairs... Privy Gallery... Banqueting House
subject: Palace of Whitehall
note: The main residence of the English monarchs in London from 1530 until 1698. It was a sprawling, disorganized complex of over 1,500 rooms.

quote: more than a few Jesuits
subject: Jesuits
note: Members of the Society of Jesus. In 17th-century England, they were often feared and suspected of being secret agents working to restore Catholic rule.

quote: the Queen’s Apartments, which had been turned into a sort of Portuguese nunnery
subject: Catherine of Braganza
note: The Portuguese Catholic wife of Charles II. Her court was a small enclave of Catholicism within the otherwise Protestant Whitehall.

quote: if they unsheathed their lancets
subject: Bloodletting
note: A standard medical practice of the time based on the theory of the four humours; Charles II was famously subjected to excessive bleeding and purging during his final days.

quote: Why, the Royal Society!
subject: The Royal Society
note: The UK's national academy of sciences, founded in 1660, of which Daniel Waterhouse and Isaac Newton are members.

quote: directly beneath where Charles I had had his head lopped off
subject: Charles I
note: The father of Charles II, executed in 1649 outside the Banqueting House following the English Civil War.

quote: Cromwell’s men had kept him prisoner
subject: Oliver Cromwell
note: The political and military leader who led the Parliamentarian victory in the Civil War and later ruled as Lord Protector.

quote: God forbid he should start thinking like Isaac
subject: Isaac Newton
note: The famous physicist and mathematician, known here for his tendency to look for hidden biblical and occult meanings in history.

quote: all the way back to the days of Bloody Mary
subject: Mary I of England
note: The Catholic Queen (reigned 1553–1558) known for her executions of Protestants, which forced many into exile.

quote: fled over the sea to Geneva, which was a hornets’ nest of Calvinists
subject: Calvinism
note: A major branch of Protestantism following the theology of John Calvin; Geneva was the center of this movement and a refuge for English 'Marian Exiles.'

quote: the man who had all the earmarks of the next Bloody Mary. James, the Duke of York
subject: James II
note: The Duke of York, later James II, whose Catholicism and authoritarian tendencies made him a polarizing figure compared to his brother Charles II.

quote: Bloody Mary
subject: Mary I of England
note: The Catholic Queen Mary I, known for her execution of Protestants; the comparison highlights the fear that James would return England to Roman Catholicism by force.

quote: Dissenters
subject: Dissenters
note: Protestants who separated from the Church of England, including Puritans, Quakers, and Baptists, who faced varying degrees of persecution.

quote: Devil’s Wind that had swept over England on the day Cromwell had died
subject: The Great Storm of 1658
note: A massive windstorm occurred on the night Oliver Cromwell died, which contemporaries interpreted as a supernatural omen regarding his soul or the state of the nation.

quote: John Churchill’s regiment of Guards
subject: John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough
note: A brilliant military commander and courtier who rose to prominence under James II before famously defecting to William of Orange.

quote: Whigs
subject: Whig Party
note: A political faction that opposed absolute monarchy and the Catholic James II, favoring parliamentary supremacy.

quote: Nahum Tate had recently translated into English a hundred-and-fifty-year-old poem... Syphilis, Sive Morbvs Gallicvs
subject: Syphilis: or, a Poetical History of the French Disease
note: A real 1686 translation by the Poet Laureate Nahum Tate of Girolamo Fracastoro's 1530 poem, which gave the disease its name.

quote: Anne Hyde, had apparently died of it; that Anne Hyde’s daughters, Mary and Anne
subject: Anne Hyde
note: The first wife of James II and mother of future Queens Mary II and Anne; rumors persisted that her health issues and those of her children were due to James's venereal diseases.

quote: Sir Roger L’Estrange
subject: Roger L'Estrange
note: A staunch Loyalist and the official Press Censor, known for his aggressive pamphlets against Whigs and Dissenters.

quote: Elizabeth (the Winter Queen)
subject: Elizabeth Stuart, Queen of Bohemia
note: The daughter of James I whose brief reign in Bohemia led to the name 'Winter Queen'; her descendants (the House of Hanover) eventually took the English throne.

quote: Marshal Turenne, the incomparable French general
subject: Henri de la Tour d'Auvergne, Vicomte de Turenne
note: One of the greatest military commanders in French history, under whom James II served with distinction during his exile.

quote: Old St. Paul’s
subject: Old St Paul's Cathedral
note: The medieval cathedral of London that was destroyed in the Great Fire of 1666 and replaced by Christopher Wren's masterpiece.

quote: Lord High Admiral
subject: Lord High Admiral
note: The titular head of the Royal Navy; James II held this post and was deeply involved in naval administration and reform.

quote: hereditary title of Lord High Admiral, and taken part in some rousing and bloody naval engagements against the Dutch
subject: James II
note: Before becoming King, James II was a successful naval commander during the Anglo-Dutch Wars, a series of conflicts over trade routes and maritime supremacy.

quote: the Winter Queen, had been kicked around Europe like a stuffed pig’s bladder
subject: Elizabeth Stuart, Queen of Bohemia
note: The daughter of James I, her brief reign in Bohemia (one winter) triggered the Thirty Years' War, leading to her long exile and her role as the 'matriarch' of many European royal lines.

quote: her daughter Sophie seemed to have bred true, and was carrying on the tradition
subject: Sophia of the Palatinate
note: The daughter of the Winter Queen; through the Act of Settlement 1701, she became the heiress to the British throne, making her the link to the future Hanoverian monarchs.

quote: Henrietta Maria of France, the mother of James and Charles
subject: Henrietta Maria of France
note: The Catholic queen consort of Charles I, whose religious friction with Parliament contributed to the English Civil War and whose influence on her sons James and Charles remained a point of political anxiety.

quote: suppress feral Presbyterians in their rustic conventicles
subject: The Covenanters
note: Refers to the 'Killing Time' in Scotland, where James oversaw the suppression of Presbyterian dissidents who refused to accept the state-mandated episcopal church structure.

quote: working hand-in-glove with Louis XIV, was just sickening
subject: Louis XIV
note: The 'Sun King' of France and a staunch Catholic; English Protestants feared James II would become a puppet of Louis, leading to absolute monarchy and the end of the Reformation in England.

quote: flooding half of Holland to keep the French out (as William of Orange had done)
subject: The Dutch Water Line
note: During the Franco-Dutch War in 1672 (the 'Year of Disaster'), the Dutch intentionally breached dikes to flood the land and create an impassable barrier against invading French troops.

quote: William of Orange
subject: William III of England
note: The Dutch Stadtholder and nephew/son-in-law of James II who would eventually lead the Glorious Revolution to take the English throne.

quote: the final struggle between the Whore of Babylon, a.k.a. the Roman Catholic Church
subject: Anti-Catholicism in England
note: A common polemic of the era where Protestants used the biblical 'Whore of Babylon' from the Book of Revelation as a derogatory metaphor for the Papacy.

quote: Romanists now swarming at Court with greater confidence than had ever been seen since the Reformation
subject: The Reformation
note: The 16th-century religious movement that led to the establishment of the Church of England and the break from the authority of the Pope.

quote: John Evelyn’s Diary
subject: John Evelyn
note: A contemporary of Samuel Pepys whose detailed diaries provide a primary historical account of life, culture, and politics in 17th-century England.

quote: Grenadier Guards washing their hands in the blue, wind-whipped flames of torchières
subject: Grenadier Guards
note: An elite infantry regiment of the British Army, originally formed in exile to protect the royal family during the Commonwealth.

quote: rambling in accented English to a younger officer—John Churchill
subject: John Churchill, 1st Duke of Marlborough
note: A brilliant military commander and key political figure who rose to prominence under James II but famously defected to William of Orange.

quote: Louis de Duras, a nephew of Marshal Turenne but a naturalized Englishman
subject: Louis de Duras, 2nd Earl of Feversham
note: A French-born nobleman who became a trusted military advisor to James II and commanded the royal forces during the Monmouth Rebellion.

quote: Daniel caught sight of Pepys and veered towards him
subject: Samuel Pepys
note: A naval administrator and famous diarist who was a close associate of James II in matters of the Admiralty.

quote: It was Jeffreys. His beautiful eyes, now trapped in a bloated and ruddy face
subject: Judge Jeffreys
note: George Jeffreys, the 'Hanging Judge,' known for his severity and his role in the 'Bloody Assizes' following the Monmouth Rebellion.

quote: It was Jeffreys. His beautiful eyes, now trapped in a bloated and ruddy face
subject: Judge Jeffreys
note: George Jeffreys, known as the 'Hanging Judge,' was a notoriously harsh jurist under James II, famous for his role in the Bloody Assizes.

quote: Mary Beatrice d’Este, a.k.a. Mary of Modena—James’s second wife
subject: Mary of Modena
note: The Catholic Queen consort of England whose pregnancy and the birth of her son sparked the crisis leading to the Glorious Revolution.

quote: Catherine Sedley, Countess of Dorchester... producing two of James’s innumerable bastard sons
subject: Catherine Sedley
note: A witty and famously 'homely' mistress of James II; her Protestantism and influence at court were points of political contention.

quote: Father Petre, who among other duties was responsible for bringing up all of James’s bastards
subject: Edward Petre
note: A Jesuit priest and close advisor to James II; his prominent role in the King's privy council fueled anti-Catholic sentiment in England.

quote: a Phanatique, a hashishin?
subject: Hashishin
note: The historical root of the word 'assassin,' referring to a medieval Nizari Ismaili sect; used here to denote a religious extremist or political killer.

quote: But his years at Trinity had made him accustomed to it.
subject: Trinity College, Cambridge
note: The prestigious Cambridge college where Isaac Newton and many other figures of the Scientific Revolution studied and worked.

quote: Kiss Jack Ketch and forgive him in advance.
subject: Jack Ketch
note: A notorious English executioner under Charles II and James II whose name became a generic term for the hangman.

quote: He saw Cromwell fight, Daniel! He saw Cromwell mow down a generation of Court fops.
subject: Oliver Cromwell
note: The leader of the Parliamentary forces in the English Civil War and later Lord Protector; his military discipline was legendary.

quote: Daniel Waterhouse was now in the Presence of James, the Duke of York.
subject: James II
note: The younger brother of Charles II; at this point in the narrative, he is the Duke of York and heir presumptive to the throne.

quote: Charles would already have made some witty remark by this point
subject: Charles II
note: The reigning King of England, known as the 'Merry Monarch,' currently on his deathbed during this scene.

quote: including Pepys—divided around him as if he were a stone in a stream
subject: Samuel Pepys
note: A naval administrator and famous diarist who was a key figure in the Restoration court and the Royal Society.

quote: surrounding the bed of the dying monarch, were James, Duke of York
subject: James II
note: The younger brother of Charles II, James was a converted Catholic whose succession to the throne was highly controversial and eventually led to the Glorious Revolution.

quote: the Duchess of Portsmouth, who was Charles’s primary mistress
subject: Louise de Kérouaille, Duchess of Portsmouth
note: A French noblewoman and mistress to Charles II, she acted as a key intermediary between the English court and Louis XIV of France.

quote: Father Huddlestone, a Catholic priest
subject: John Huddleston
note: A Benedictine monk who helped Charles II escape after the Battle of Worcester and was brought to the King's deathbed to receive him into the Catholic Church.

quote: Louis de Duras, the Earl of Feversham
subject: Louis de Duras, 2nd Earl of Feversham
note: A French-born nephew of Turenne who became a naturalized English peer and served as a loyal military commander for both Charles II and James II.

quote: Father Petre was shoulder-to-shoulder with Barrillon, the French Ambassador
subject: Edward Petre
note: A Jesuit priest and close advisor to James II; his influence was a major source of Protestant anxiety regarding the 'popish' influence on the Crown.

quote: Barrillon, the French Ambassador
subject: Paul Barillon
note: The long-serving French ambassador to England who facilitated secret subsidies from Louis XIV to the English monarchy.

quote: working his way through the closing stanzas of the rite of extreme unction
subject: Extreme Unction
note: The Catholic sacrament of anointing the sick or dying; Charles II's deathbed conversion and receipt of this rite were politically explosive in Protestant England.

quote: You know I’m a Freedom of Conscience man
subject: Freedom of Conscience
note: A 17th-century political and religious concept advocating for the right to practice one's religion without state interference, often used by James II to justify toleration for Catholics.

quote: SAMUEL PEPYS
subject: Samuel Pepys
note: A naval administrator and famous diarist who was a key figure in the administration of the Admiralty and a member of the Royal Society.

quote: DANIEL DEFOE, Moll Flanders
subject: Daniel Defoe
note: English writer and spy, best known for 'Robinson Crusoe'. 'Moll Flanders' (1722) is a novel about a woman navigating the precarious social and economic realities of the late 17th century.

quote: To M. le comte d’Avaux
subject: Jean-Antoine de Mesmes, Comte d'Avaux
note: A veteran French diplomat and ambassador to the Dutch Republic (The Hague) during the 1680s, known for his extensive spy network.

quote: the King of France’s brother: namely, a fascination with women’s undergarments
subject: Philippe I, Duke of Orléans
note: Known as 'Monsieur', the younger brother of Louis XIV. He was famously flamboyant, often dressed in women's clothing, and maintained a court that contrasted with the King's more formal atmosphere.

quote: King Louis XIV... considers his bastards to be only one small step beneath the Dauphin
subject: Louis XIV
note: The 'Sun King' of France who centralized power at Versailles. He took the unusual step of legitimizing his children by his mistresses, granting them high social rank.

quote: the daughter of a ruined petty noble... married a stunted and crippled writer in Paris
subject: Françoise d'Aubigné, Marquise de Maintenon
note: Born in a prison and first married to the disabled poet Paul Scarron, she eventually became the governess to Louis XIV's illegitimate children and later his secret second wife.

quote: This Duchess was none other than the maîtresse déclarée to the King himself
subject: Françoise-Athénaïs de Rochechouart, Marquise de Montespan
note: The most celebrated mistress of Louis XIV, she bore him seven children and dominated the court until she was supplanted by Madame de Maintenon.

quote: the Dauphin and the other Enfants de France
subject: Enfants de France
note: A specific rank at the French court for the legitimate children of the King and the Dauphin (the heir apparent), carrying immense prestige and protocol requirements.

quote: your tedious duties in the Hague
subject: The Hague
note: The seat of government in the Dutch Republic and a center of international diplomacy and espionage in the 17th century.

quote: JOHN BUNYAN, The Pilgrim’s Progress
subject: John Bunyan
note: A Puritan preacher and author. 'The Pilgrim's Progress' (1678) is one of the most significant works of English literature, an allegory of the Christian soul's journey.

quote: To Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: German polymath and philosopher who co-invented calculus; he spent significant time in Paris and was deeply involved in the intellectual and political life of Europe.

quote: Difficulty at the beginning
subject: I Ching
note: A reference to Hexagram 3 of the ancient Chinese 'Book of Changes'. Leibniz was historically fascinated by the I Ching and its binary patterns.

quote: harim of the Topkapi Palace in Constantinople
subject: Topkapi Palace Harem
note: The secluded living quarters for the wives, concubines, and female relatives of the Ottoman Sultan, known for its complex internal hierarchy and political influence.

quote: the Salon of Apollo and view the Sun King in his radiance
subject: Salon d'Apollon
note: The throne room at Versailles, named after the Greek sun god with whom Louis XIV identified himself.

quote: At Topkapi there were magnificent carpets everywhere
subject: Topkapi Palace
note: The primary residence and administrative headquarters of the Ottoman Sultans in Istanbul, representing the height of Ottoman power and luxury.

quote: making a study of family trees for Sophie
subject: Sophia of the Palatinate
note: Electress of Hanover and a key figure in the Hanoverian Succession; she was a patron of Leibniz and a granddaughter of James I of England.

quote: M. le comte de Béziers was given the honor of holding a candle during the King’s going-to-bed ceremony
subject: Le Lever and Le Coucher
note: The highly ritualized morning and evening ceremonies of Louis XIV where courtiers competed for the honor of performing menial tasks for the King to gain proximity and favor.

quote: detected the key from the I Ching
subject: I Ching
note: An ancient Chinese divination text. Leibniz was famously fascinated by it, seeing in its hexagrams a precursor to his binary numeral system.

quote: If what they say about Colbert is true... he died two years ago
subject: Jean-Baptiste Colbert
note: The First Minister of State under Louis XIV who reformed French finances and promoted mercantilism, science, and the arts.

quote: I am writing in that broken cypher to d’Avaux in Holland
subject: Jean-Antoine de Mesmes, Comte d'Avaux
note: A prominent French diplomat and ambassador to the Dutch Republic during the late 17th century.

quote: Since you employ the Wilkins cypher
subject: John Wilkins
note: A polymath and founding member of the Royal Society who wrote 'Mercury, or the Secret and Swift Messenger', a treatise on cryptography.

quote: Louis XIV chose most of his most important ministers... from the middle class
subject: Louis XIV
note: The 'Sun King' of France whose reign epitomized absolute monarchy and the centralization of power at Versailles.

quote: bought one of your digital computers!
subject: Leibniz's Stepped Reckoner
note: A digital mechanical calculator invented by Leibniz around 1672, the first to perform all four arithmetic operations.

quote: he has (secretly) married a woman of low degree
subject: Françoise d'Aubigné, Marquise de Maintenon
note: The second wife of Louis XIV, married in a private ceremony; she rose from poverty to become the most powerful woman at court.

quote: the King banned the mobile from all of his gardens
subject: The Mobile
note: A 17th-century term for the 'mobile vulgus' or the fickle crowd/common people, from which the modern word 'mob' is derived.

quote: The King’s brother Philippe, who is generally addressed as Monsieur
subject: Philippe I, Duke of Orléans
note: The younger brother of Louis XIV, known for his military skill as well as his flamboyant effeminacy and preference for male favorites.

quote: as a young girl in Algiers... by the Ladies’ Volunteer Sodality of the Society of Britannic Abductees
subject: Barbary Pirates and White Slavery
note: The historical practice of North African privateers capturing Europeans for the slave markets of the Maghreb, a major concern for 17th-century maritime powers.

quote: I recognized him as King Louis XIV.
subject: Louis XIV
note: The 'Sun King' of France, whose reign (1643–1715) represents the height of absolute monarchy and French cultural dominance in Europe.

quote: ranting to the children about Descartes and Euclid
subject: René Descartes
note: A French philosopher and mathematician whose rationalist philosophy and coordinate geometry were foundational to the scientific revolution.

quote: Elisabeth Charlotte, known to Versailles as Madame, and known to Sophie—her beloved aunt—as Liselotte.
subject: Elizabeth Charlotte, Madame Palatine
note: The Duchess of Orléans and sister-in-law to Louis XIV, famous for her extensive and blunt correspondence detailing life at the French court.

quote: her husband Philippe is a homosexual
subject: Philippe I, Duke of Orléans
note: The younger brother of Louis XIV; while a successful military commander, he was famously flamboyant and open about his preference for male favorites.

quote: that girl who was duped by d’Avaux
subject: Jean-Antoine de Mesmes, Comte d'Avaux
note: A key French diplomat and ambassador during the reign of Louis XIV, involved in major negotiations like the Treaty of Nijmegen.

quote: To Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: A polymath and philosopher who co-invented calculus; in the novel, he is a mentor and correspondent to Eliza.

quote: I am sorry that your Spanish uncle had to move to Amsterdam
subject: Crypto-Jews in Amsterdam
note: Refers to Sephardic Jews (often 'New Christians' or Marranos) who fled the Spanish Inquisition to Amsterdam, where they became central to international trade and finance.

quote: their fathers and grandfathers used what money they had to raise private armies and fortify their cities
subject: The Fronde
note: A series of civil wars in France (1648–1653) where nobles rebelled against royal authority; Louis XIV’s move to Versailles was partly to domesticate these nobles.

quote: apartments of a Lady in Waiting to the Dauphine
subject: The Dauphine
note: The title given to the wife of the Dauphin (the heir apparent to the French throne); at this time, it refers to Maria Anna Victoria of Bavaria.

quote: the French East India Company there around 1666
subject: French East India Company
note: Established by Jean-Baptiste Colbert in 1664 to compete with the British and Dutch, it struggled with mismanagement and conflict in the Indian Ocean trade.

quote: the city was in the process of falling to the Mahratta rebels
subject: Maratha Empire
note: The Marathas, led by Shivaji, rose against the Mughal Empire in the 17th century; their 1664 and 1670 sacks of the port of Surat were major blows to European and Mughal trade.

quote: Shahjahanabad, where they hoped to beg for succor from the Great Mogul Aurangzeb
subject: Aurangzeb
note: The sixth Mughal Emperor (reigned 1658–1707), known for expanding the empire to its greatest extent but also for his religious orthodoxy and constant warfare.

quote: the Red Fort at Agra
subject: Agra Fort
note: A massive 16th-century Mughal fortress that served as the main residence of the emperors until the capital shifted to Delhi (Shahjahanabad).

quote: strangled by dacoits
subject: Dacoity
note: A term derived from the Hindi 'dakait' referring to armed bandits; in this period, they were a significant hazard for travelers on Indian trade routes.

quote: The Queen of France died two years ago
subject: Maria Theresa of Spain
note: The first wife of Louis XIV, who died in 1683. Her death allowed the King to secretly marry his mistress, Madame de Maintenon.

quote: The King has Mme. de Maintenon now, but she is not officially his wife.
subject: Madame de Maintenon
note: The second wife of Louis XIV, married in a secret ceremony around 1683; she exerted significant moral and political influence over the later years of his reign.

quote: the Dauphine, wife of the King’s eldest son
subject: Maria Anna Victoria of Bavaria
note: The wife of Louis, Grand Dauphin; as the highest-ranking woman at court after the Queen's death, her household was the center of female political maneuvering.

quote: living altar during black masses held at an abandoned country church
subject: Affair of the Poisons
note: A major scandal (1677–1682) involving a series of murders and alleged Satanic rituals among the high nobility of Louis XIV's court.

quote: instituted the chambre ardente to investigate these doings
subject: Chambre Ardente
note: A special extraordinary court of justice in France, most famously convened by Louis XIV to prosecute the Affair of the Poisons.

quote: Mme. de Maintenon opens her school for poor girls of the French nobility at St. Cyr
subject: Maison royale de Saint-Louis
note: A school founded in 1684 by Madame de Maintenon to provide an education for the daughters of impoverished noblemen who had died in the King's service.

quote: Mme. de Maintenon opens her school for poor girls of the French nobility at St. Cyr
subject: Madame de Maintenon
note: The secret second wife of Louis XIV, she was a powerful figure at court who founded the Maison royale de Saint-Louis at Saint-Cyr to educate impoverished noble girls.

quote: the King will spend October at Fontainebleu as is his custom
subject: Palace of Fontainebleau
note: A primary residence of French monarchs; Louis XIV's court followed a rigid seasonal schedule of moving between various royal châteaus.

quote: To M. le comte d’Avaux 25 September 1685
subject: Jean-Antoine de Mesmes, Comte d'Avaux
note: A key French diplomat and ambassador to the Dutch Republic during the reign of Louis XIV.

quote: some common people had erected a cantilever... filled with dozens of stray cats... brought here to amuse the crowd
subject: Cat-burning
note: A form of ritual entertainment in early modern France (and elsewhere) where cats were burned alive, often during midsummer festivals, believed to bring good luck or ward off evil.

quote: During the dismal years of the Fronde Rebellion
subject: The Fronde
note: A series of civil wars in France (1648–1653) where the nobility rose against the crown; the trauma of being hunted by mobs during his childhood deeply influenced Louis XIV's absolute rule.

quote: King Louis XIV of France
subject: Louis XIV
note: The 'Sun King' who centralized French power at Versailles and became the archetype of the absolute monarch.

quote: bills of exchange or little purses of diamonds
subject: Bills of Exchange
note: A primary instrument of international trade and finance in the 17th century, acting as a precursor to modern checks and paper money.

quote: a Jesuit priest named Édouard de Gex
subject: Jesuits
note: The Society of Jesus, a Catholic religious order known for its political influence, educational rigor, and role as confessors to European monarchs.

quote: the King’s getting-out-of-bed ceremony
subject: Le Lever
note: The highly ritualized morning ceremony where the King of France was dressed and prepared for the day in front of selected courtiers.

quote: two likely candidates being the Dauphine... and Liselotte
subject: Liselotte (Elizabeth Charlotte, Madame Palatine)
note: The sister-in-law of Louis XIV, known for her blunt, voluminous letters that provide a detailed and often cynical account of life at the French court.

quote: the most famous chirurgeon in Paris
subject: Chirurgeon (Surgeon)
note: In this era, surgeons were distinct from physicians, performing manual operations and often associated with the barber-surgeon guild.

quote: le Roi
subject: Louis XIV
note: Louis XIV, the 'Sun King,' ruled France from 1643 to 1715, establishing an absolute monarchy and moving the royal court to Versailles.

quote: D’Avaux says you are good with money
subject: Jean-Antoine de Mesmes, Comte d'Avaux
note: A prominent French diplomat and ambassador during the reign of Louis XIV, known for his service in Venice, Sweden, and the Dutch Republic.

quote: Colbert was a commoner. He was good with money; he built everything you see.
subject: Jean-Baptiste Colbert
note: The First Minister of State under Louis XIV; he was a brilliant administrator who reformed French finances and promoted the mercantilist policies that funded the construction of Versailles.

quote: I saw a frightful purplish swelling in the crevice of his buttocks.
subject: Louis XIV's Anal Fistula
note: In 1686, Louis XIV underwent a famous and agonizing surgery to repair an anal fistula. The success of the operation by surgeon Charles-François Félix elevated the status of surgery in France.

quote: Even among your fellow Jesuits you are respected
subject: Jesuits
note: The Society of Jesus, a Catholic religious order known for its rigorous education, missionary work, and political influence as confessors to European monarchs.

quote: To Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: A German polymath and philosopher who co-invented calculus; he is a central figure in the novel's intellectual landscape.

quote: the Protestant heretics who infest his realms
subject: Huguenots
note: French Protestants who faced increasing persecution under Louis XIV, culminating in the 1685 Edict of Fontainebleau which revoked their religious freedoms.

quote: Any tooth-puller chosen at random from the Pont-Neuf
subject: Pont-Neuf
note: The oldest standing bridge across the river Seine in Paris, which in the 17th century was a bustling center of commerce, street performers, and 'tooth-pullers' (early dentists).

quote: d’Aquin, the King’s doctor, got it wrong, and the resulting wound became abscessed
subject: Antoine d'Aquin
note: The personal physician to Louis XIV from 1672 to 1693, whose medical failures (including the disastrous dental surgery described) eventually led to his disgrace.

quote: Louis seized the Franche-Comté in 1667
subject: War of Devolution
note: The conflict in which Louis XIV first attempted to claim the Spanish Netherlands and Franche-Comté, marking the beginning of his long series of expansionist wars.

quote: Mme. de Maintenon, who looks to him as a sort of spiritual guide
subject: Françoise d'Aubigné, Marquise de Maintenon
note: The devout second wife of Louis XIV (married secretly) who exerted significant influence over the King's religious policies and the moral climate of the court.

quote: the R.P.R., the religion prétendue réformée, or Huguenots
subject: Huguenots
note: French Protestants who faced increasing persecution under Louis XIV, culminating in the revocation of the Edict of Nantes.

quote: the route joining Genoa and Lyons—the financial aorta of Christendom
subject: The Spanish Road
note: A vital military and trade supply route used by the Spanish and other powers to move troops and wealth through Europe, bypassing hostile French territory.

quote: English Puritans fled for refuge during the reign of Bloody Mary
subject: Marian Exiles
note: Protestants who fled England during the reign of Mary I (1553–1558) to escape religious persecution, many of whom settled in Geneva and brought back radical Calvinist ideas.

quote: Anne Marie, the daughter of Monsieur by his first wife, Minette of England
subject: Henrietta of England (Minette)
note: The youngest daughter of Charles I of England and sister to Charles II; her marriage to 'Monsieur' (the Duke of Orléans) linked the English and French royal houses.

quote: Calvinist proselytizers would come up the valleys to preach to the common folk
subject: The Waldensians
note: A pre-Reformation Christian movement in the Alpine valleys of Savoy and France that later aligned with Calvinism and faced brutal military suppression.

quote: Maréchal de Catinat at its head
subject: Nicolas Catinat
note: A prominent French military commander under Louis XIV known for his campaigns in Italy and the suppression of religious minorities in the Alps.

quote: Il Saggiatore (THE ASSAYER) IN Opere, v. 6, p. 197
subject: The Assayer
note: A seminal 1623 book by Galileo Galilei regarding the scientific method and the use of mathematics to understand the universe.

quote: Robert Hooke, Fellow of the Royal Society
subject: Robert Hooke
note: A brilliant but famously cantankerous polymath and rival of Newton who served as the Royal Society's Curator of Experiments.

quote: mixing up his dinner: a compound of mercury, iron filings, flowers of sulfur, purgative waters
subject: alchemy
note: Hooke was known for self-medicating with various chemicals and recording the effects; this reflects the era's blurred lines between chemistry, medicine, and alchemy.

quote: campaigning like a French regiment through every whorehouse in Whitechapel
subject: Whitechapel
note: A district in East London that was historically known for poverty, overcrowding, and vice outside the city walls.

quote: Newton? ... from the time we began rooming together until my ejection in ’77
subject: Isaac Newton
note: The passage references Newton's reclusive and obsessive nature during his years at Trinity College, Cambridge.

quote: concocted the Elixir Vitae and is immortal now
subject: alchemy
note: The 'Elixir of Life' was a mythical alchemical substance supposed to grant eternal life or youth.

quote: third helping of usquebaugh
subject: Usquebaugh
note: An early form of whiskey (from the Gaelic 'uisce beatha' or 'water of life').

quote: natural philosophy as opposed to unnatural theology
subject: Natural Philosophy
note: The precursor to modern science; Newton spent as much (if not more) time on radical theological studies and biblical chronology as he did on physics.

quote: Leibniz has come to London for the second time!
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: The German polymath who independently co-invented calculus, leading to a bitter priority dispute with Newton.

quote: Oldenburg is furious with him because he has failed to bring the digital computer
subject: Henry Oldenburg
note: The first Secretary of the Royal Society; he managed a vast network of scientific correspondence across Europe.

quote: failed to bring the digital computer, as promised!
subject: Computing Machines
note: Leibniz invented the 'Stepped Reckoner,' a mechanical calculator that could perform all four arithmetic operations.

quote: De Motu Corporum in Gyrum. Halley says it is stupendous.
subject: De Motu Corporum in Gyrum
note: Newton's 1684 manuscript ('On the motion of bodies in an orbit') which provided the mathematical foundation for universal gravitation.

quote: Halley has an explanation for the orbit of his comet
subject: Edmond Halley
note: The astronomer who used Newton's laws to predict the return of the comet that now bears his name.

quote: And with Flamsteed around to depress the statistics
subject: John Flamsteed
note: The first Astronomer Royal, known for his meticulous observations and his frequent professional clashes with Newton and Halley.

quote: Louise de Kéroualle and her cleavage had hove into view
subject: Louise de Kéroualle
note: A mistress of Charles II and a French spy, she wielded significant political influence at the English court.

quote: Louise de Kéroualle and her cleavage had hove into view
subject: Louise de Kéroualle
note: A mistress of Charles II and a French spy, she exerted significant political influence at the English court.

quote: finding the Longitude by plotting the motions of the moon against the stars
subject: The Longitude Problem
note: The 'Lunar Distance' method was a primary scientific theory for determining a ship's east-west position at sea.

quote: Christopher Wren
subject: Christopher Wren
note: The famed architect of St. Paul's Cathedral and a founding member of the Royal Society with a background in astronomy.

quote: They had asked one John Flamsteed.
subject: John Flamsteed
note: The first Astronomer Royal, known for his meticulous star catalogs and his eventual conflicts with Newton and Halley.

quote: Charles II had appointed him Astronomer Royal and founded the Royal Observatory.
subject: Royal Observatory
note: Established in 1675 at Greenwich to improve navigation and solve the longitude problem through astronomical observation.

quote: calculating the precise date and hour of the Apocalypse, based upon occult shreds of data from the Bible
subject: Apocalypticism
note: Newton spent decades studying biblical prophecy and chronology, believing the end of the world could be calculated mathematically.

quote: Halley shows up and has a conversation with Newton
subject: Edmond Halley
note: The astronomer who famously predicted the return of the comet that bears his name and who persuaded Newton to publish his work on gravity.

quote: Within a few months he’s got De Motu out.
subject: De Motu Corporum in Gyrum
note: Newton's short manuscript sent to Halley in 1684, which provided the mathematical foundation for orbital mechanics and preceded the Principia.

quote: devising new receipts for the Philosophic Mercury
subject: Alchemy
note: Newton's secret alchemical research focused on 'Philosophic Mercury,' a substance believed to be essential for the transmutation of metals.

quote: The ball transfers its vis viva into another ball
subject: Vis Viva
note: An early term for kinetic energy (literally 'living force'), which was at the center of a major debate in 17th-century physics regarding conservation laws.

quote: it describes a Galilean trajectory, a parabola.
subject: Galilean Trajectory
note: Galileo's discovery that projectiles follow a parabolic path, a cornerstone of early modern kinematics.

quote: It is the problem that Descartes could not solve.
subject: René Descartes
note: The French philosopher whose 'vortex theory' of planetary motion Newton eventually sought to disprove with his theory of universal gravitation.

quote: It is the problem that Descartes could not solve.
subject: René Descartes
note: A French philosopher and mathematician whose mechanical philosophy and coordinate geometry provided the foundation—and the limitations—that Newton and Leibniz sought to transcend.

quote: Mr. Hooke! Roger said. 'M’lord.' 'You, sir, taught this fellow to make thermometers!'
subject: Robert Hooke
note: The Curator of Experiments for the Royal Society and a polymath who made significant improvements to scientific instruments, including thermometers and microscopes.

quote: The French and the Papists are running the country now
subject: James II
note: Refers to the reign of the Catholic King James II, whose pro-French and pro-Catholic policies created intense political and religious tension in England.

quote: Isaac had soared far beyond Hooke’s reach since Halley’s visit.
subject: Edmond Halley
note: The astronomer who visited Newton in 1684 to discuss orbital mechanics, a meeting that famously prompted Newton to write the 'Principia Mathematica'.

quote: And as the Sun King was now pouring much gold into England
subject: Louis XIV
note: Louis XIV of France, known as the Sun King, who used his immense wealth to subsidize the English monarchy (Charles II and James II) to keep England aligned with French interests.

quote: any half-wit with a back-staff and an ephemeris could sail unerringly
subject: Backstaff
note: A navigational instrument used to measure the altitude of the sun by observing its shadow, allowing sailors to determine their latitude.

quote: It was a copy of Acta Eruditorum, the scholarly rag that Leibniz had founded
subject: Acta Eruditorum
note: The first scientific journal of the German-speaking lands, founded in 1682, which served as the primary venue for Leibniz's mathematical publications.

quote: Acta Eruditorum was Leibniz’s (hence Germany’s) answer to Journal des Savants
subject: Journal des Savants
note: The earliest academic journal published in Europe (established 1665), representing the intellectual output of the French scientific community.

quote: Published the calculus!
subject: The Calculus Priority Dispute
note: Refers to Leibniz's 1684 publication 'Nova Methodus pro Maximis et Minimis', which beat Newton to print and sparked a decades-long controversy over who invented calculus first.

quote: King James II that His Majesty’s claims to support all religious dissidents
subject: James II
note: James II, a Catholic king, issued Declarations of Indulgence to suspend penal laws against both fellow Catholics and Protestant Dissenters, a move that was politically controversial.

quote: take Cromwell’s skull down from the stick where it had been posted
subject: Oliver Cromwell
note: After the Restoration, Cromwell's body was exhumed and posthumously executed; his head was displayed on a spike at Westminster Hall for decades.

quote: Newton seems to agree with you... or else he’s afraid that some Jesuit will say so
subject: Jesuits
note: The Society of Jesus (Jesuits) were often viewed with suspicion and fear in Protestant England as agents of the Pope and masters of casuistry.

quote: like the æther filling the void between particles of matter
subject: Aether
note: In 17th-century physics, the aether was a hypothesized medium that filled all space to explain how forces like light and gravity could travel through a vacuum.

quote: like something from the Geneva Bible
subject: Geneva Bible
note: The primary Bible of 16th-century English Protestantism, favored by Puritans for its anti-authoritarian marginal notes, even after the King James Version was released.

quote: consider these, er, infinitesimals and evanes-cents in a way that is rigorous
subject: Infinitesimals
note: The mathematical concept of quantities so small they cannot be measured, which was central to the development of calculus and sparked intense philosophical debate.

quote: Leibniz agrees—and the result... is here, and it is called the calculus
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: Leibniz independently developed calculus and published his version (using notation still used today) before Newton published his own.

quote: applying just the approach you have prescribed, in the mathematical realm, is here, and it is called the calculus
subject: The Calculus Priority Dispute
note: The intellectual battle between Newton and Leibniz over who first invented calculus is a central theme of the novel.

quote: cheap stuff from India and Dutch shipping that could bring it to Europe
subject: Calico Acts and Textile Trade
note: The influx of cheap, high-quality Indian cottons (calicoes) threatened the traditional English wool and cloth industries, leading to protectionist laws.

quote: enforce the Penal Laws, all of Suffolk’s most outstanding Quakers, Barkers, Ranters, Congregationalists, Presbyterians
subject: Penal Laws
note: A series of laws that upheld the rites of the Church of England and punished 'nonconformists' (dissenting Protestants) and Catholics with fines and imprisonment.

quote: Quakers, Barkers, Ranters, Congregationalists, Presbyterians
subject: English Dissenters
note: Various Protestant groups that separated from the Church of England; they differed in their views on church hierarchy, sacraments, and social radicalism.

quote: most clipped, worn, filed-down, and adulterated coins
subject: The Great Recoinage of 1696
note: Before milled edges were standard, people 'clipped' silver from the edges of coins, leading to a currency crisis where the face value of money far exceeded its silver weight.

quote: showed up at Stourbridge Fair peddling this or that
subject: Stourbridge Fair
note: One of the largest and most famous fairs in Europe, held near Cambridge; it was a massive center of commerce and a gathering place for all social classes.

quote: the Restoration had occurred and that Europe was in fact ruled by great Kings
subject: Charles II
note: The return of the monarchy in 1660 after the English Civil War and the Interregnum, marking a shift back to traditional royal authority.

quote: he was a close advisor to King James II
subject: James II
note: The Catholic brother of Charles II whose reign was marked by religious tension and the eventual Glorious Revolution.

quote: go to a meeting of the Royal Society
subject: The Royal Society
note: The premier scientific institution of the era, founded in 1660 to promote 'experimental learning'.

quote: supposing that we’ll rally about his standard as if he really gives a farthing for Freedom of Conscience!
subject: Declaration of Indulgence
note: James II's 1687 attempt to establish religious toleration in England, which many Protestants viewed as a cynical move to favor Roman Catholics.

quote: I have met the Duke of Monmouth
subject: Duke of Monmouth
note: The illegitimate son of Charles II who led a failed rebellion against James II in 1685.

quote: To say nothing of Oliver Cromwell.
subject: Oliver Cromwell
note: The Lord Protector who led the Parliamentarian forces and ruled England as a republic after the execution of Charles I.

quote: Greeks, e.g., Euclid, had done all of these things long ago
subject: Euclid
note: The ancient Greek mathematician known as the 'father of geometry' whose 'Elements' served as the main textbook for mathematics for centuries.

quote: LIBRI I. CONICORVM APOLLONII
subject: Apollonius of Perga
note: A Greek geometer whose work 'Conics' defined the properties of the ellipse, parabola, and hyperbola.

quote: written out by John Flamsteed, the Astronomer Royal
subject: John Flamsteed
note: The first Astronomer Royal of England, who cataloged over 3,000 stars and had a famously contentious relationship with Newton.

quote: Newton had requested information concerning the comet of 1680
subject: Great Comet of 1680
note: The first comet discovered by telescope; its path was crucial for Newton to prove that comets followed the same laws of gravity as planets.

quote: a recent conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn
subject: Great Conjunction
note: The alignment of Jupiter and Saturn, which provided data for studying gravitational perturbations between planets.

quote: the ebb and flow of tides in the ocean.
subject: Theory of Tides
note: One of the major phenomena Newton sought to explain using his law of universal gravitation and the influence of the moon.

quote: John Flamsteed had stuck his neck out by about ten miles and asked the question, What if this was not two comets but one?
subject: John Flamsteed
note: The first Astronomer Royal, Flamsteed's meticulous observations were crucial to Newton's work, though the two men famously feuded over the release of Flamsteed's data.

quote: the supposed two comets of 1680 might not have been one comet
subject: Great Comet of 1680
note: The first comet discovered by telescope, it was used by Newton to test Kepler's laws and prove that comets followed conic sections.

quote: Kepler and Copernicus
subject: Johannes Kepler and Nicolaus Copernicus
note: Key figures of the Scientific Revolution; Copernicus proposed the heliocentric model, and Kepler established the laws of planetary motion.

quote: Titan (the moon of Saturn that Huygens had discovered)
subject: Christiaan Huygens
note: A prominent Dutch mathematician and physicist who discovered Titan and invented the pendulum clock.

quote: all that mattered was how much gold they could fetch on the Damplatz
subject: Dam Square (Damplatz)
note: The historical heart of Amsterdam and its commercial center, home to the Town Hall and the Exchange.

quote: Wren’s library had started building
subject: Wren Library
note: Designed by Christopher Wren in 1676, this library at Trinity College, Cambridge, is a masterpiece of Baroque architecture.

quote: the Duke of Tweed—a.k.a. General Lewis, the L in Charles II’s CABAL
subject: The CABAL Ministry
note: A group of five high-ranking ministers (Clifford, Arlington, Buckingham, Ashley, and Lauderdale) who dominated English policy between 1668 and 1674; 'Tweed' is a fictionalized version of Lauderdale.

quote: If they moved on conic sections, it was no accident.
subject: Conic Sections
note: The curves (ellipse, parabola, hyperbola) obtained by intersecting a cone with a plane; Newton proved planetary orbits follow these shapes.

quote: A comet following a precise hyperbolic trajectory through the æther
subject: Aether
note: A substance once thought to fill all space, serving as the medium through which light and gravitational forces were transmitted.

quote: traversing the pounded, shit-permeated mud flat where Stourbridge Fair was held
subject: Stourbridge Fair
note: One of the largest fairs in Europe, held annually near Cambridge; it was a major center of commerce where Newton famously bought his first prisms.

quote: The notion that the Sun exerted some centripetal force on the planets
subject: Centripetal Force
note: The 'center-seeking' force Newton identified as necessary to keep planets in curved orbits rather than flying off in straight lines.

quote: Charles II had kicked out the Puritan scholars who had nested there under Wilkins
subject: John Wilkins
note: A founding member of the Royal Society and Master of Trinity College during the Interregnum, known for his work on universal language and science.

quote: The question of aggregates had been vexing Leibniz to no end.
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: A German polymath and philosopher whose theory of 'monads' addressed how simple substances form complex aggregates.

quote: Hooke had found that the human body was made up of cells
subject: Robert Hooke
note: The Royal Society's Curator of Experiments who coined the term 'cell' after observing cork under a microscope in his 1665 book Micrographia.

quote: these High Church gentles would never come out and say what they were thinking about religion
subject: High Church
note: A faction within the Church of England that emphasized ritual, the authority of bishops, and continuity with Catholic tradition, often associated with Tory politics.

quote: Remember, our King is a Catholic.
subject: James II
note: The passage refers to James II, who succeeded his brother Charles II in 1685; his open Catholicism caused immense political tension in Protestant England.

quote: this was Vigani, the alchemist.
subject: John Francis Vigani
note: An Italian chemist who became the first Professor of Chemistry at Cambridge; he was a real-life associate of Newton until they fell out over a joke about a nun.

quote: considering what King Louis does to Huguenots
subject: Huguenots
note: French Protestants who were severely persecuted under Louis XIV, especially after the 1685 Revocation of the Edict of Nantes, leading to a mass exodus.

quote: Spain with the Inquisition
subject: The Spanish Inquisition
note: A judicial institution established to maintain Catholic orthodoxy in Spain, notorious in England as a symbol of Catholic tyranny.

quote: Freedom of Conscience is the King’s byword
subject: Declaration of Indulgence
note: James II issued declarations in 1687 and 1688 attempting to establish religious toleration for Catholics and Protestant Dissenters, which many Anglicans saw as a move to undermine the Church of England.

quote: the one who has been spotted in the Hague?
subject: William III of Orange
note: The Dutch Stadtholder based in the Hague, who was the primary Protestant alternative to James II and eventually took the English throne in the Glorious Revolution.

quote: Ever since Mr. Halley came up to enquire on the subject of Comets
subject: Edmond Halley
note: The astronomer who famously visited Newton in 1684 to discuss orbital mechanics, a visit that prompted Newton to write the Principia Mathematica.

quote: GEMELLI CARERI
subject: Giovanni Francesco Gemelli Careri
note: An Italian adventurer and traveler (1651–1725) famous for his world travels, which he funded by 'carrying' goods to avoid customs, and his book Giro del Mondo.

quote: Isaac’s furnaces were cold, the stuff in their crucibles congealed.
subject: alchemy
note: Isaac Newton spent decades of his life practicing alchemy, seeking the 'Philosopher's Stone' and the 'Philosophick Mercury' alongside his work on physics.

quote: curves were evanescent and fluxional
subject: calculus-priority-dispute
note: Newton's version of calculus was called the 'Method of Fluxions,' where he viewed variables as 'flowing' quantities.

quote: Sir Robert Moray gazing at a stuffed eel in a glass box
subject: Sir Robert Moray
note: A Scottish statesman and polymath who was a primary founder of the Royal Society and a close confidant of Charles II.

quote: a box of golden guineas left open next to a window
subject: Guinea (coin)
note: The guinea was a gold coin minted in England between 1663 and 1814, named for the region in West Africa where much of the gold was sourced.

quote: addressed to JEOVA SANCTUS UNUS
subject: Jeova Sanctus Unus
note: An anagram of 'Isaacus Neuutonus' (Isaac Newton) used as a pseudonym; it translates to 'Jehovah Holy One,' reflecting Newton's private anti-trinitarian religious views.

quote: dimensions of the Tabernacle to Moses and Temple with its Courts to David & Ezekiel
subject: Solomon's Temple
note: Newton was obsessed with the architecture of Solomon's Temple, believing its floor plan encoded the structure of the universe and the 'System of the World.'

quote: the world started out perfectly good and has gotten worse and worse since then
subject: apocalypticism
note: Newton and many of his contemporaries believed in 'Prisca Sapientia'—the idea that ancient civilizations possessed lost, perfect knowledge that had since decayed.

quote: But the Apocalypse did not come in 1666.
subject: great-fire-of-london
note: The year 1666 was widely feared as the year of the Apocalypse due to the 'Number of the Beast' (666) and the occurrence of the Great Plague and Great Fire.

quote: I am working out the System of the World upstairs
subject: Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica
note: Newton's 'System of the World' refers to the third book of his 'Principia,' which applied his laws of motion and gravity to the entire universe.

quote: In ’77, Boyle was certain he had it.
subject: Robert Boyle
note: A founding member of the Royal Society and 'father of chemistry' who, like Newton, was deeply involved in alchemical research.

quote: You have read my De Motu Corporum in Gyrum?
subject: De Motu Corporum in Gyrum

note: The principle stating that a physical quantity (like gravity) is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source; it was the key to Newton's law of universal gravitation.

quote: The Hague
